name: Danilo Pérez
abstract: Danilo Pérez (born December 29, 1965) is a Panamanian pianist, composer, educator, and a social activist. His music is a blend of Panamanian roots with elements of Latin American folk music, jazz, European impressionism, African, and other musical heritages that promote music as a multi-dimensional bridge between people.

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: This became a co-led project... we toured Europe with the quartet... we played a sold-out week in New York at The Blue Note
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Palmieri
about_quotes.evidence: Danilo Perez, down the line, they all... You’ve got to go through a Keith Jarrett. You’ve got to go through a Chick Corea, which we’re friends... I love Chick. I don’t know Keith, but I know Herbie Hancock.
about_quotes.doc_title: Eddie Palmieri — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ramsey Lewis
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER: If I think about – at one point there was an influx of a lot of Latin players, like Danilo Pérez . . . INTERVIEWEE: Whom I love.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ramsey Lewis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: David Finck
about_quotes.evidence: In Paquito’s band we also had a drummer who was very strong with that stuff, and a pianist — I think that’s Danilo Perez on that.
about_quotes.doc_title: David Finck interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, May 29, 2015

about_quotes.speaker: Steve Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Adam Cruz is always on the road with different people, you know Danilo Perez, or, he’s always on different stuff.
about_quotes.doc_title: Steve Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 25, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Wayne Shorter
about_quotes.evidence: Danilo and I, about a month ago, we did a Skype thing... We’re going to go to Panama in January for his festival.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wayne Shorter — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
